metadata.paper_title: Towards a Broader View on Software Architecture Analysis of Flexibility
metadata.venue: Proceedings of the First Working IFIP Conference on Software Architecture

system_context.domain: Administrative System for Military Training Centers
system_context.system_description: A system for registration of data concerning courses and students, used by fifteen training centers of the Dutch army.
system_context.business_goals: Registration of data concerning courses and students.

evaluation_setup.evaluation_goal: To assess the flexibility of a large administrative system and gain insight into the factors that influence the complexity of changes.

architectures.name: MISOC2000
architectures.description: Divided into macro architecture (environment) and micro architecture (internal structure). Micro architecture is layered (client, server, database) and subsystem-based.

key_components.responsibilities: Formulating course catalogs and production plans.
Distribution of course catalogs and recording agreements with customers.
Management of the availability of locations and items.
Exporting cost information to KIO and importing information about cost centers from KIO.
An extension of the P-module to record personnel information specific for training centers.
Administrative purposes, like maintenance of authorization and configuration data.
Logs users in to and out of MISOC2000; registers user with database.

assumptions: Systems owned by training centers can be changed autonomously; those owned by others require coordination.
LAN2000 standard sets the configuration of client and server machines.

scenarios.scenario_text: What happens when a branch of military service replaces Windows NT 4.0 by Windows 2000?
What happens when the DoD changes the operating system in LAN2000 from Windows NT 4.0 to Unix (for both workstations and servers)?
What happens when a new version of COOL:Gen is used for MISOC2000?
What happens when the authorization client is changed?
What happens when the user interface style of the P-module is changed?
What happens when the external supplier changes the interface style of the timetable component?
What happens when PICO is used for transferring course results to the P-module of the organizational unit of a student?
What happens when the processes of the training centers are changed?
What happens when a number of services have to cooperate in one training center?
What happens when training centers have to share their assets (locations, vehicles, etc.)?

scenario_classification.rationale: Requires regeneration and recompilation of the system.
Requires regeneration, recompilation, and potential reimplementation of platform-specific components (OCX).
Requires regeneration, recompilation, testing, and deployment of all systems created with COOL:Gen.
MISOC2000 is unaffected by changes to the authorization client that do not affect its database.
Causes inconsistencies in interaction style but system logic remains functional (though adaptation is difficult due to template restrictions).
No impact on MISOC2000 as it is not compulsory to use the new version.
MISOC2000 has to be adapted to automatically export results; specifically the 'Student' subsystem.
Requires modification of the division in subsystems (micro architecture).
MISOC2000 is unaffected; data entered as local data.
Would require connecting instances of MISOC2000 (enormous impact on macro architecture) or handling outside the system.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Regenerate and recompile system for specific service (undesirable) or follow LAN2000 standard.
Regenerate and compile for new platform; replace/rewrite OCX component.
issues_identified: Dependencies with other systems make changing technical environment very hard

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Upgrade run-time files; regenerate, recompile, test and deploy all COOL:Gen systems.
issues_identified: Ripple effect to all other COOL:Gen systems (P-module, O-module, etc.) due to shared run-time files

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Adapt user interface elements by hand.
issues_identified: Very difficult to adapt style of all UI elements because initial template cannot be changed once project is created

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Adapt to automatically export results to PICO.
issues_identified: Only one subsystem has to be adapted

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Connect instances of MISOC2000 at various training centers.

scenario_interactions.modularity_implication: Shared run-time files cause dependencies between MISOC2000, P-module, O-module, and Authorization client, forcing simultaneous upgrades.

architectural_strengths: Server applications do not use platform-specific features (portability)
Independence from some external systems (GOOS, KIO, MARS) due to file transfer or database separation
Centralized link to PICO in 'Student' subsystem
architectural_weaknesses: Strong dependencies on P-module and O-module due to call relationships
Use of OCX-control limits portability to UNIX
Shared COOL:Gen run-time files create lock-step upgrade requirements
Inability to change COOL:Gen template after project creation hinders UI adaptation

recommendations: View software architecture at two levels: micro (internal) and macro (environment/relations).
Consider ownership as a factor in flexibility (multiple owners increase complexity).
Be aware that standardization (e.g., LAN2000) sacrifices flexibility for reduced complexity.

inference_notes: Inferred 'Indirect' classification for scenarios described as requiring regeneration, recompilation, or architectural changes.
Inferred 'Direct' classification for scenarios described as having no impact or only causing inconsistencies without strictly requiring code changes.
Ground truth scenarios list covers all 10 scenarios explicitly numbered in the paper.
